sources.notes: Operation decreases the y smallest selected elements where y is the selected sum modulo the selected set size.
Official reference code tests a terminal congruence condition, with a special n>23 equality shortcut, then tests the state after decrementing the smallest element once.

annotation.summary: Given a modulo-based decrement operation on arbitrary subsets, find the minimum achievable total sum or report that the sum can decrease indefinitely.
annotation.constraints: n can be up to 1e6 and values up to 1e9, so the answer must be characterized by global modular invariants rather than simulation.
annotation.core_idea: A finite minimum is reached only when every subset size operation has zero remainder effect: the total sum is divisible by n, and all elements have the same residue modulo every k from 2 to n-1. For n>23 this residue condition forces equality within the value range used by the solution. The official solution checks whether the current sorted array is terminal; otherwise it decrements the smallest element once and checks again. If neither state is terminal, some positive-remainder operation remains and the sum can be decreased without bound.
annotation.complexity: O(n log n + n * min(n, 23)) per test case.

solution_variants.name: congruence-terminal-test
solution_variants.summary: Sort the array, test the modular terminal condition for the current state and after one decrement of the minimum element; otherwise output -1.

tags.evidence: The terminal-state test is expressed through divisibility of the total sum and equal residues modulo all subset sizes.
The solution classifies finite answers by a global invariant-like terminal congruence condition rather than by simulating operations.
Sorting identifies the element to decrement in the single fallback check and fixes tie order for the operation's smallest-elements rule.
The implementation separates n>23 because checking all moduli is replaced by the stronger all-equal condition.
